data.content:
### Build Frontend and Start Development Server

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/AGENTS.md

Commands to build the frontend application and start the local development server.

```shell
pnpm --filter=@posthog/frontend build
./bin/start
```

--------------------------------

### Start Full Development Stack with hogli

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This command initiates the complete PostHog development environment, including the backend, plugin server, workers, and frontend. It utilizes the underlying 'bin/start' script orchestrated by 'mprocs'.

```bash
hogli start
```

--------------------------------

### Start PostHog Development Environment with Docker Compose

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/manual-dev-setup.md

Initiates the PostHog development environment by starting the services defined in the `docker-compose.dev.yml` file. This command spins up all necessary external services and the PostHog application itself for local development.

```bash
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up
```

--------------------------------

### Python Click Command for Developer Workflows

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogli/README.md

This snippet demonstrates how to add a custom Python Click command for developer workflows. It resides in `common/hogli/commands.py` and allows for complex Python logic, argument parsing, and multi-step Python operations.

```python
# In common/hogli/commands.py
from hogli.core.cli import cli
import click

@cli.command(name="my-workflow", help="Custom workflow command")
@click.option('--verbose', is_flag=True, help='Verbose output')
def my_workflow(verbose: bool) -> None:
    """Custom workflow implementation."""
    # Your Python logic here
    click.echo("Running custom workflow...")
```

--------------------------------

### Install Python 3.12 on Debian-based Linux

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/manual-dev-setup.md

Installs Python 3.12 and its development files on Debian-based Linux systems using a PPA. Ensures the correct Python version is available.

```bash
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:deadsnakes/ppa -y
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3.12 python3.12-venv python3.12-dev -y
```

--------------------------------

### Run Development Server using npm

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/hedgebox-dummy/README.md

Starts the development server for the Next.js application. The app will be accessible at http://localhost:3000, provided the PostHog API key is correctly set up.

```bash
npm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### Install sqlx-cli and Run Migrations

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/manual-dev-setup.md

Installs the SQLx CLI tool for database migrations and then executes the migration script. This sets up the necessary database schema.

```bash
cargo install sqlx-cli # If you haven't already
DEBUG=1 ./bin/migrate
```

--------------------------------

### Start Development Server (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/wiki/integrations/JS-integration

Starts the development server for PostHog. This command is used to build and serve a minified production version of the PostHog JavaScript library.

```bash
yarn start
```

--------------------------------

### Local Toolbar Testing Setup

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/products/product_tours/ARCHITECTURE.md

Instructions for setting up a local development environment to test the PostHog toolbar, which runs within an iframe.

```shell
hogli start
```

```shell
cd frontend && pnpm build
```

--------------------------------

### Manage PostHog Worktrees with `phw` command (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/flox-multi-instance-workflow.md

Provides bash commands for managing PostHog development worktrees. These commands facilitate creating new branches with isolated environments, checking out existing branches, switching between worktrees, and reviewing pull requests.

```bash
# Create a NEW branch with a worktree
phw create haacked/new-feature
# You're now IN the worktree with Flox activated!
bin/start
# Access at http://localhost:8000

# Or specify a different base branch
phw create haacked/new-feature master
```

```bash
# Work on an EXISTING branch by creating a worktree if it doesn't exist
phw checkout haacked/new-feature
# Creates worktree for existing branch, Flox activated!
bin/start
# Access at http://localhost:8000
```

```bash
# Switch to an EXISTING worktree
phw switch haacked/new-feature
# Switches to already created worktree, Flox activated!
bin/start
# Access at http://localhost:8000
```

```bash
# Review a Pull Request
phw pr 12345
# Fetched PR, switched to worktree, ready to test!
bin/start
# Access at http://localhost:8000
```

```bash
# List all worktrees
phw list

# Remove a specific worktree
phw remove pr-5678-teammate
```

--------------------------------

### Install SAML Dependencies on Debian-based Linux

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/manual-dev-setup.md

Installs required libraries for SAML functionality on Debian-based Linux distributions. This includes XML parsing and crypto libraries.

```bash
sudo apt install -y libxml2 libxmlsec1-dev libffi-dev pkg-config
```

--------------------------------

### Dynamic Content Generation (Jinja2)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/frontend/src/index.html

This snippet uses Jinja2 templating to dynamically generate strings for display. It conditionally sets text based on variables like 'debug', 'region', 'git_branch', and 'git_rev'. This is commonly used for build information or environment-specific messages. The output is intended to be integrated into other parts of the application, such as the JavaScript console log or HTML content.

```jinja2
{% include "head.html" %} {% include "overlays.html" %}
{% include "live_server_inject.html" %}
```

--------------------------------

### Start PostHog Development Server

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/README.md

Run the PostHog development server with the DEBUG flag enabled. This command is used for local testing and development of data sources and integrations.

```bash
DEBUG=1 ./bin/start
```

--------------------------------

### Define and Orchestrate Daily Trace Clustering Workflow (Python)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/posthog/temporal/llm_analytics/trace_clustering/README.md

Defines the `DailyTraceClusteringWorkflow` class which orchestrates three separate Temporal activities sequentially. It calculates window bounds based on the `lookback_days` parameter.

```python
from temporalio import workflow

# Assuming activities are defined in activities.py
from . import activities

@workflow.defn
class DailyTraceClusteringWorkflow:
    @workflow.run
    async def run(self, team_id: int, lookback_days: int, max_samples: int, min_k: int, max_k: int) -> None:
        window_start, window_end = await workflow.execute_activity(
            activities.calculate_window_bounds, 
            (lookback_days,), 
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(seconds=10)
        )

        await workflow.execute_activity(
            activities.perform_clustering_compute_activity, 
            (team_id, window_start, window_end, max_samples, min_k, max_k),
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=2), # 120s
            retry_policy=RetryPolicy(initial_interval=timedelta(seconds=5), backoff_coefficient=2, maximum_interval=timedelta(seconds=30), maximum_attempts=3)
        )

        await workflow.execute_activity(
            activities.generate_cluster_labels_activity, 
            (team_id, window_start, window_end),
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5), # 300s
            retry_policy=RetryPolicy(initial_interval=timedelta(seconds=10), backoff_coefficient=2, maximum_interval=timedelta(seconds=60), maximum_attempts=2)
        )

        await workflow.execute_activity(
            activities.emit_cluster_events_activity, 
            (team_id, window_start, window_end),
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(seconds=60), # 60s
            retry_policy=RetryPolicy(initial_interval=timedelta(seconds=5), backoff_coefficient=2, maximum_interval=timedelta(seconds=30), maximum_attempts=3)
        )

```

--------------------------------

### Add Backend Models and Migrations (Python)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/products/README.md

Place backend models within the product's `backend/` folder and use direct imports. Employ string-based foreign key references to prevent circular imports. Create a `migrations` folder and generate initial migrations using `python manage.py makemigrations your_product_name -n initial_migration`. For moved models, use `migrations.SeparateDatabaseAndState` to manage `DROP TABLE` and `CREATE TABLE` operations carefully.

```python
# products/your_product_name/backend/models.py
from django.db import models

class YourModel(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=255)
    # Use string-based foreign key references
    team = models.ForeignKey("posthog.Team", on_delete=models.CASCADE)

    class Meta:
        # If moving a model, specify the old table name
        db_table = 'old_table_name'
        managed = True

```

```python
# products/your_product_name/backend/migrations/0001_initial_migration.py
from django.db import migrations, models

class Migration(migrations.Migration):

    dependencies = [
        ('posthog', '0001_initial'),
    ]

    operations = [
        migrations.CreateModel(
            name='YourModel',
            fields=[
                ('id', models.BigAutoField(auto_created=True, primary_key=True, serialize=False, verbose_name='ID')),
                ('name', models.CharField(max_length=255)),
                ('team', models.ForeignKey(on_delete=models.CASCADE, to='posthog.Team')),
            ],
            options={
                'db_table': 'old_table_name',
                'managed': True,
            },
        ),
    ]

```

```python
# Example of migrations.SeparateDatabaseAndState for moving models
# In posthog/migrations/0548_migrate_early_access_features.py
from django.db import migrations

class Migration(migrations.Migration):
    # ... dependencies and database_operations ...
    state_operations = [
        # Operations to create the model in the new app's state
    ]

# In products/early_access_features/migrations/0001_initial_migration.py
from django.db import migrations

class Migration(migrations.Migration):
    # ... dependencies and database_operations ...
    state_operations = [
        # Operations to create the model in the new app's state
    ]

```

--------------------------------

### Set Environment for React Markdown in JavaScript

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/frontend/src/exporter/index.html

Ensures that a global `window.process` object exists with a `cwd` function, which is a requirement for `react-markdown` library to function correctly in certain environments. This snippet provides a fallback implementation if `window.process` is not already defined.

```javascript
if (!window.process) {
  window.process = {
    cwd: () => ''
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Create Django Migration (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/developing-locally.md

Generates a new Django migration file. The `DEBUG=1` environment variable is used, which may influence migration behavior or logging. This command initiates the migration creation process.

```bash
DEBUG=1 ./manage.py makemigrations
```

--------------------------------

### One-Time Setup for PostHog Development (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/docs/published/flox-multi-instance-workflow.md

Installs necessary tools (direnv, gh, jq) and configures the shell environment for PostHog development using Flox and the `phw` function. This setup enables automatic directory changes and Flox environment activation.

```bash
# Install dependencies
brew install direnv gh jq

# Add direnv hook to your shell (~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc)
eval "$(direnv hook zsh)"  # or bash

# Add the phw function for auto-cd functionality
echo 'source ~/dev/posthog/posthog/bin/phw' >> ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc

# Reload your shell
source ~/.zshrc  # or ~/.bashrc

# Verify setup in main repo
cd ~/dev/posthog/posthog
flox activate
# You should see Flox environment activate and uv sync run
```

--------------------------------

### Python C API: Building Values with Py_BuildValue

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/common/hogql_parser/CONTRIBUTING.md

Explains how to construct Python objects like tuples, dictionaries, and lists using the `Py_BuildValue()` function in the Python C API. It also provides best practices, such as using `s#` format specifier for C++ strings to avoid `strlen` and using the `N` format specifier for passing object references when ownership is transferred.

```c
#include <Python.h>
#include <string>

void example_build_values() {
    // Building a tuple: (1, "hello")
    PyObject* tuple_val = Py_BuildValue("(is)", 1, "hello");
    Py_XDECREF(tuple_val);

    // Building a dictionary: {"key": "value"}
    PyObject* dict_val = Py_BuildValue("{s:s}", "key", "value");
    Py_XDECREF(dict_val);

    // Building a list: [1, 2, 3]
    // For dynamic lists, PyList_New() is preferred over Py_BuildValue with repeated calls
    PyObject* list_val = PyList_New(3);
    if (list_val) {
        PyObject* item1 = Py_BuildValue("i", 1);
        PyObject* item2 = Py_BuildValue("i", 2);
        PyObject* item3 = Py_BuildValue("i", 3);

        PyList_SET_ITEM(list_val, 0, item1); // Steals reference to item1
        PyList_SET_ITEM(list_val, 1, item2); // Steals reference to item2
        PyList_SET_ITEM(list_val, 2, item3); // Steals reference to item3
    }
    Py_XDECREF(list_val);

    // Using s# for C++ strings (avoids strlen)
    std::string cpp_string = "world";
    PyObject* str_val_s = Py_BuildValue("s", cpp_string.c_str()); // Uses strlen
    PyObject* str_val_s_hash = Py_BuildValue("s#", cpp_string.c_str(), cpp_string.length()); // Preferred
    Py_XDECREF(str_val_s);
    Py_XDECREF(str_val_s_hash);

    // Using N for passing object references (creates new reference)
    PyObject* existing_obj = PyLong_FromLong(10);
    if (existing_obj) {
        // If you no longer need existing_obj, pass it with N
        PyObject* tuple_with_n = Py_BuildValue("(N)", existing_obj); 
        // existing_obj refcount is not incremented by Py_BuildValue here
        Py_DECREF(existing_obj); // Still need to DECREF the original
        Py_XDECREF(tuple_with_n);
    }
}

```

--------------------------------

### Start PostHog with 1Password Credentials

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog/blob/master/ee/api/vercel/README.md

This command initiates the PostHog development servers while securely loading Vercel integration credentials from 1Password via the `.env.vercel` file. It ensures that both Django and Vite development servers are started with all necessary environment variables.

```bash
op run --env-file=.env.vercel -- bin/start
```
